A/B Testing: A controlled experiment comparing two model versions by routing a portion of live traffic to each. Measures which model performs better on real business metrics before full rollout.
A100 GPU: NVIDIA A100 — a high-end data center GPU with 40GB or 80GB VRAM. Designed for demanding training and inference workloads. Common in cloud GPU rentals for LLM fine-tuning.
ACID: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — properties guaranteed by relational database transactions. Atomicity: all-or-nothing. Consistency: valid state transitions. Isolation: concurrent transactions don't interfere. Durability: committed data survives crashes.
AI: Artificial Intelligence — the broad field of creating machines capable of performing tasks that typically require human intelligence, including reasoning, learning, perception, and language understanding.
API: Application Programming Interface — a set of defined rules and protocols that allows different software applications to communicate with each other. REST APIs use HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) over endpoints like /api/users.
API Key: A secret token used to authenticate requests to third-party services (OpenAI, Hugging Face, Tavily). Must be stored securely as environment variables, never in source code.
AWS: Amazon Web Services — the world's largest cloud computing platform, offering 200+ services across compute (EC2, Lambda), storage (S3, EBS), databases (RDS, DynamoDB), networking (VPC, CloudFront), and ML (SageMaker, Bedrock).
Accuracy: Overall fraction of correct predictions: (TP + TN) / (TP + TN + FP + FN). Can be misleading for imbalanced datasets (e.g., 99% accurate model that always predicts 'no fraud' when fraud is 1% of data).
Activation Function: A non-linear function applied to neuron outputs that enables neural networks to learn complex patterns. ReLU (most common), GELU (used in Transformers), Sigmoid, Tanh.
Adam: Adaptive Moment Estimation — a popular gradient descent variant combining momentum and adaptive learning rates per parameter. AdamW adds weight decay regularization. Default optimizer for most LLM fine-tuning.
Agent: An AI system that can autonomously take actions using tools (APIs, code execution, database queries) in pursuit of a goal. Combines an LLM 'brain' with a set of tools and a reasoning loop (e.g., ReAct).
Agile: Iterative software development methodology emphasizing flexibility, collaboration, and customer feedback. Scrum: sprints (1-4 weeks), daily standups, retrospectives. Kanban: visual boards, WIP limits, continuous flow. Contrasts with Waterfall (sequential, plan-everything-upfront).
Attention: A mechanism in Transformers that computes weighted relevance scores between all pairs of tokens in a sequence, allowing the model to dynamically focus on important context regardless of distance.
Auto Scaling: Automatically adjusting compute capacity to maintain performance and optimize costs. AWS Auto Scaling Groups: launch/terminate EC2 instances based on metrics (CPU, request count). Application Auto Scaling: scales ECS services, DynamoDB throughput, and Aurora replicas.
BERT: Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers — a landmark 2018 encoder-only model pre-trained with masked language modeling. Foundation for many NLP understanding tasks.
BM25: Best Matching 25 — a probabilistic keyword-based ranking function used in traditional search engines. Compensates for term frequency saturation and document length. Often combined with vector search in hybrid retrieval.
Babel: A JavaScript compiler that transforms modern JavaScript (ES6+) into backwards-compatible versions for older browsers. Plugin-based architecture. Also transforms JSX and TypeScript. Integrated into most build tools (Webpack, Vite).
Backpropagation: The algorithm for computing gradients of the loss function with respect to all network weights by applying the chain rule backward through the network. Enables efficient training of deep neural networks.
Batch Size: The number of training examples processed before model weights are updated. Larger batches give more stable gradients but require more GPU memory. Gradient accumulation simulates larger batches.
Bi-Encoder: A model architecture that encodes query and document separately into embeddings. Fast for retrieval at scale (can pre-compute document embeddings) but less precise than cross-encoders.
Bias in AI: Systematic errors or unfair predictions caused by flawed assumptions or imbalances in training data, model architecture, or deployment. Can perpetuate or amplify societal biases if not addressed.
Bias-Variance Tradeoff: The fundamental tension in ML between underfitting (high bias) and overfitting (high variance). Bias = error from oversimplification. Variance = error from sensitivity to training data noise.
CAP Theorem: In a distributed system, you can only guarantee two of: Consistency (all nodes see same data), Availability (every request gets a response), Partition Tolerance (system works despite network failures). Since partitions are inevitable, you choose CP or AP.
CDK: AWS Cloud Development Kit — an Infrastructure as Code framework using familiar programming languages (TypeScript, Python, Java) to define cloud infrastructure. Synthesizes to CloudFormation templates. Higher-level than raw CloudFormation with reusable Constructs.
CDN: Content Delivery Network — a geographically distributed network of proxy servers. Caches static assets (images, CSS, JS) close to users, reducing latency. CloudFront (AWS), Cloudflare, Fastly. Also provides DDoS protection, TLS termination, and edge compute (CloudFront Functions, Lambda@Edge).
CI/CD: Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery (or Deployment) — a DevOps practice where code changes are automatically tested (CI) and deployed to staging/production (CD). CI/CD pipelines automate build, test, and deploy steps. Tools: GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins.
CI/CD Pipeline: An automated sequence of steps: Build → Test → Staging → Production. In AWS: CodeCommit (source) → CodeBuild (build/test) → CodeDeploy (deploy). Can integrate with GitHub/GitLab. Includes automated rollbacks on failure.
CI/CD for ML: Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment pipelines adapted for ML — automating model training, evaluation, and deployment with proper gates (performance thresholds, fairness checks).
CLI: Command Line Interface — a text-based interface for interacting with software. aws-cli manages AWS resources from terminal. git is a CLI for version control. CLIs are scriptable and automate-able unlike GUIs. Often faster for experienced users.
CNN: Convolutional Neural Network — a neural network architecture specialized for grid-structured data (images, video). Uses convolutional filters to learn spatial hierarchies of features. Revolutionized computer vision.
CORS: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — a browser security mechanism controlling which web pages can access resources from different origins. Server sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers. Without CORS, the Same-Origin Policy blocks cross-origin requests.
CORS Error: Browser blocks a cross-origin request because the server didn't include proper Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers. Common during local development when frontend (localhost:3000) calls backend (localhost:8080). Fix: configure CORS middleware on the server.
CRUD: Create, Read, Update, Delete — the four basic operations of persistent storage. Maps to HTTP methods: POST (Create), GET (Read), PUT/PATCH (Update), DELETE (Delete). Maps to SQL: INSERT, SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE.
CSRF: Cross-Site Request Forgery — an attack that tricks a user's browser into making unwanted requests to a site they're authenticated with. Prevention: CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies, checking Origin/Referer headers. Modern frameworks often handle this automatically.
CSS: Cascading Style Sheets — stylesheet language for describing the presentation of HTML documents. Controls layout, colors, fonts, and responsiveness. Modern features: Grid, Flexbox, Custom Properties (variables), Container Queries.
CUDA: Compute Unified Device Architecture — NVIDIA's parallel computing platform and API for GPU programming. Required for PyTorch/TensorFlow GPU acceleration.
CV: Computer Vision — the field of AI that enables machines to interpret and understand visual information from the world (images, video).
Cache: A hardware or software component storing data for faster future requests. Web caching: CDN (edge locations), browser cache, Redis/Memcached (application cache). Cache strategies: write-through, write-behind, cache-aside. Cache invalidation is notoriously difficult.
Callback: A function passed as an argument to another function, to be executed later. Common in event handlers, async operations, and array methods like forEach/map/filter. Callback hell (deeply nested callbacks) was the problem Promises and async/await solved.
Chain-of-Thought: A prompting technique where the model is instructed to reason step-by-step before producing a final answer. Dramatically improves performance on math, logic, and multi-step reasoning tasks.
ChromaDB: An open-source vector database designed for LLM applications. Lightweight, runs in-process (Python), with persistence to disk. Ideal for prototyping and small-to-medium RAG applications.
Chunking: The process of splitting long documents into smaller, overlapping segments before embedding. Critical for RAG — chunk size and overlap strategy directly impact retrieval quality.
Circuit Breaker: A design pattern that prevents cascading failures in distributed systems. Monitors for failures; after a threshold, 'opens the circuit' (fails fast) for a cooldown period, then attempts to reset. States: Closed (normal), Open (failing), Half-Open (testing recovery). Libraries: resilience4j, opossum (Node.js).
Classification: A supervised learning task of predicting discrete category labels. Binary (spam/not spam), multi-class (animal species), and multi-label (multiple tags per item).
Closure: A function that retains access to its lexical scope even when executed outside that scope. JavaScript closures are created every time a function is created. Used for data privacy, currying, and the module pattern. Functions remember the environment where they were created.
CloudFormation: AWS's native Infrastructure as Code service. Define infrastructure in JSON or YAML templates, CloudFormation provisions and manages the resources. Templates are declarative. CDK synthesizes to CloudFormation. Supports drift detection.
CloudWatch: AWS monitoring service: collects logs, metrics, and events from AWS resources and applications. Features: Logs (centralized logging), Metrics (CPU, memory, custom), Alarms (threshold-based notifications), Dashboards, and Contributor Insights. Foundation of observability on AWS.
Clustering: An unsupervised learning task of grouping similar data points together. K-Means (fast, needs pre-specified cluster count), DBSCAN (density-based, handles arbitrary shapes), hierarchical clustering.
Cold Start: The latency overhead when a container/function needs to initialize before serving requests. Serverless ML deployments face this when model loading takes seconds to minutes.
CommonJS: The module system used by Node.js originally: require() to import, module.exports to export. Synchronous, dynamically resolved at runtime. Still widely used but being replaced by ESM for new projects.
Component Lifecycle: Phases of a React component: Mount (constructor, render, componentDidMount), Update (render, componentDidUpdate), Unmount (componentWillUnmount). With Hooks: useEffect replaces lifecycle methods. Cleanup function in useEffect = componentWillUnmount.
Computer Vision: The AI subfield focused on enabling machines to interpret and understand visual information from images and video. Tasks include classification, object detection, segmentation, and generation.
Concept Drift: A type of model drift where the fundamental relationship between inputs and outputs changes. Example: what constitutes 'spam email' evolves as spammers change tactics.
Confusion Matrix: A table showing TP, TN, FP, FN counts. Foundation for computing precision, recall, accuracy, and F1 score. Reveals which classes the model confuses.
Connection Pool: A cache of database connections maintained so they can be reused. Creating new connections is expensive. Pool size controls max concurrent connections. Exhausting the pool causes requests to queue. Libraries: pg-pool, mysql2 pool, HikariCP (Java).
Container: A lightweight, standalone executable package including code, runtime, system tools, and libraries. More portable and efficient than VMs (shares host OS kernel). Docker standardized containers. OCI (Open Container Initiative) ensures portability across runtimes.
Context Window: The maximum number of tokens an LLM can process in a single forward pass. Includes both the input prompt and generated output. Modern models range from 4K to 1M+ tokens. Also called context length.
Contextual Retrieval: Anthropic's technique where each chunk is prepended with document context (title, summary of surrounding chunks) before embedding. Improves retrieval accuracy by adding broader context to each chunk.
Continuous Batching: An optimization where incoming requests are batched dynamically as they arrive (not waiting for a fixed batch), maximizing GPU utilization and throughput for LLM serving.
Cosine Similarity: A measure of similarity between two vectors, computed as the cosine of the angle between them. Ranges from -1 (opposite) to 1 (identical). The standard metric for comparing embeddings.
Cost Optimization: Strategies to reduce LLM operational costs: use smaller/cheaper models for simple tasks, cache frequent responses, minimize prompt length, batch requests when possible.
Cross-Encoder: A model architecture that processes two input sequences together (concatenated) and outputs a single relevance score. More accurate than bi-encoders for ranking, but slower — used as a re-ranker in RAG pipelines.
Cross-Validation: A technique for robustly estimating model performance by splitting data into K folds, training on K-1 folds and testing on the held-out fold, repeating K times. K-fold cross-validation is the standard approach.
DARE: Drop And REscale — a model merging technique that randomly drops delta parameters and rescales remaining ones to preserve expected magnitude. Reduces interference when merging multiple models.
DBSCAN: Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise — a clustering algorithm that groups densely packed points and marks sparse regions as noise. No need to pre-specify cluster count.
DL: Deep Learning — a subset of ML using multi-layered neural networks to learn hierarchical representations from data. Powers modern LLMs, computer vision, and speech recognition.
DNS: Domain Name System — the phonebook of the internet. Translates human-readable domain names (google.com) into IP addresses (142.250.185.46). Uses hierarchical distributed database with root, TLD, and authoritative nameservers.
DOM: Document Object Model — a programming interface for HTML and XML documents. Represents the page as a tree of nodes that JavaScript can manipulate. The Virtual DOM (React) optimizes updates by batching and diffing changes before touching the real DOM.
DPO: Direct Preference Optimization — a simpler alternative to RLHF that directly optimizes the model from preference pairs without needing a separate reward model or RL. More stable and efficient.
Data Augmentation: Artificially expanding training data by applying transformations (e.g., rotating images, paraphrasing text, adding noise) to improve model generalization and robustness.
Data Drift: A type of model drift where the distribution of input features changes from what the model was trained on. Example: user demographics shifting, requiring model retraining.
Data Privacy: Protecting sensitive information in ML systems through techniques like differential privacy (adding calibrated noise to training data), federated learning (training on distributed data), and data anonymization.
Decision Tree: A tree-structured model that splits data based on feature values to make predictions. Highly interpretable but prone to overfitting. Building block for ensemble methods.
Decoder-Only Model: A Transformer variant using only the decoder stack. GPT, Llama, and Claude are decoder-only. Autoregressive: generates text left-to-right, predicting the next token. Dominant architecture for modern LLMs.
Deserialization: The reverse of serialization — reconstructing an object from a stored or transmitted format. Security concern: deserializing untrusted data can lead to remote code execution. Always validate and sanitize deserialized data.
DevOps: A culture and set of practices combining software development (Dev) and IT operations (Ops). Aims to shorten the development lifecycle and provide continuous delivery with high software quality. Key concepts: automation, monitoring, collaboration.
Differential Privacy: A mathematical framework that provides strong privacy guarantees by adding calibrated noise to data or model outputs. Ensures that inclusion or exclusion of a single individual's data doesn't significantly change results.
Diffusion Model: A generative model that learns to reverse a gradual noising process, generating high-quality images from random noise. Powers DALL-E, Stable Diffusion, and Midjourney.
Docker: A containerization platform that packages applications and their dependencies into portable, isolated containers. Containers share the host OS kernel but run in isolated user spaces. Defined by Dockerfiles; orchestrated by Docker Compose or Kubernetes.
Docker Compose: Tool for defining and running multi-container Docker applications. Uses docker-compose.yml to define services, networks, and volumes. Single command (docker compose up) starts the entire stack. Ideal for local development environments.
Dropout: A regularization technique that randomly deactivates a fraction of neurons during each training step, forcing the network to learn redundant representations and reducing overfitting.
DynamoDB: Amazon's fully managed NoSQL key-value and document database. Single-digit millisecond performance at any scale. Features: auto-scaling, global tables (multi-region), DynamoDB Streams (change data capture), DAX (in-memory cache). Partition key + optional sort key model.
EC2: Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud — virtual servers (instances) in the cloud. Configurable CPU, memory, storage, and networking. Pricing models: On-Demand, Reserved, Spot (up to 90% cheaper but interruptible). Foundation of most AWS deployments.
ES6/ES2015: A major JavaScript update (2015) introducing: let/const, arrow functions, classes, template literals, destructuring, default parameters, rest/spread operators, Promises, modules (import/export), Map, Set, WeakMap, WeakSet.
ESM: ECMAScript Modules — the official JavaScript module system using import/export syntax. Supported natively in modern browsers and Node.js (with 'type': 'module' in package.json). Replaces CommonJS (require/module.exports) as the standard.
ETag: Entity Tag — an HTTP response header used for cache validation. A unique identifier for a specific version of a resource. Client sends If-None-Match with the ETag; server responds 304 Not Modified if unchanged. More flexible than Last-Modified dates.
Embedding: A dense vector (array of floats) representing a piece of text, image, or other data in a high-dimensional space. Semantically similar items have vectors that are close together (measured by cosine similarity).
Encoder-Decoder: An architecture pattern where an encoder maps input to a latent representation and a decoder generates output from it. Used in machine translation (original Transformer), image captioning, and T5 models.
Encoder-Only Model: A Transformer variant using only the encoder stack. BERT is the canonical example. Good for understanding tasks: classification, NER, and embedding generation.
Encryption at Rest: Protecting stored data by encrypting it when written to disk. AES-256 is the industry standard. AWS: S3 server-side encryption (SSE-S3, SSE-KMS), EBS volume encryption, RDS encryption. Transparent to applications when using managed services. Critical for compliance (GDPR, HIPAA).
Encryption in Transit: Protecting data as it travels across networks using TLS/HTTPS. Prevents eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks. Certificates (X.509) authenticate the server (and optionally the client via mTLS). Modern best practice: encrypt everything, everywhere.
Environment Variables: Configuration values set outside the application (os.environ / process.env). Used for secrets (API keys, DB passwords), environment-specific config (dev/staging/prod URLs), and feature flags. Never commit .env files to version control.
Epoch: One complete pass through the entire training dataset during model training. Multiple epochs are usually needed, but too many can cause overfitting.
Event Loop: The mechanism that allows Node.js (and browsers) to perform non-blocking I/O operations despite JavaScript being single-threaded. Continuously checks the call stack; if empty, processes callbacks from the task queue and microtask queue. Microtasks (Promises) execute before macrotasks (setTimeout).
Event-Driven Architecture: An architectural pattern where services communicate through events (state changes). Components: event producers, event routers (event bus/message broker), event consumers. AWS services: EventBridge, SNS, SQS. Enables loose coupling and real-time reactivity.
EventEmitter: A Node.js pattern where objects emit named events to which functions (listeners) can subscribe. Core to Node's async architecture. Used in: HTTP servers (request event), streams (data event), and custom modules. Similar to the Observer/Observable pattern.
Eventual Consistency: A consistency model where after an update, all reads will eventually return the updated value, but not necessarily immediately. Tradeoff for high availability in distributed systems (AP in CAP theorem). Used by: DynamoDB (default), S3, DNS. Contrasts with strong consistency.
F1 Score: The harmonic mean of precision and recall. Ranges from 0 to 1. F1 = 2 × (precision × recall) / (precision + recall). Best single metric for imbalanced classification problems.
FP16: 16-bit floating point (half precision). Used for faster training and inference on GPUs with Tensor Cores. Reduces memory usage by 2× compared to FP32 with minimal accuracy impact.
FP32: 32-bit floating point (full precision). The default for model weights before quantization. Higher precision but more memory and slower computation.
Fairness in ML: Ensuring ML models don't discriminate against protected groups. Requires measuring and mitigating bias in training data, model predictions, and outcomes. Techniques: fairness constraints, reweighting, adversarial debiasing.
FastAPI: A modern, high-performance Python web framework for building APIs. Popular for serving ML models due to async support, automatic OpenAPI docs, and validation with Pydantic.
Feature Engineering: The process of selecting, transforming, and creating input features from raw data to improve model performance. Less critical with deep learning (which learns features automatically) but still vital for tabular/structured data problems.
Feature Store: A centralized platform for storing, sharing, and serving ML features. Solves the problem of feature inconsistency between training and serving. Examples: Feast, Tecton.
Federated Learning: A privacy-preserving ML technique where models are trained across decentralized devices/servers holding local data, without the data ever leaving its origin. Aggregated model updates, not raw data, are shared.
Few-Shot Prompting: A prompt engineering technique where you provide 2-5 examples of input-output pairs in the prompt, teaching the model the desired pattern without any weight updates.
Fine-Tuning: The process of further training a pre-trained model on a smaller, domain-specific dataset to adapt its behavior. Contrasts with prompt engineering (no weight changes) and pre-training (training from scratch).
Formatter: A tool that automatically formats code to a consistent style. Prettier (JS/TS/CSS/HTML/JSON), Black (Python), gofmt (Go). Unlike linters, formatters focus exclusively on style (spacing, line breaks, quotes). Use both: formatter for style, linter for logic issues.
Function Calling: An LLM API feature where the model outputs structured JSON indicating which function to call and with what arguments. The calling application executes the function and returns results. Also called tool calling.
Functional Programming: A paradigm treating computation as evaluation of mathematical functions, avoiding mutable state and side effects. Key concepts: pure functions, immutability, higher-order functions, recursion. Languages: Haskell, Clojure, Elm. Modern JS/TS supports FP patterns (map, filter, reduce).
GAN: Generative Adversarial Network — two neural networks (generator and discriminator) compete: the generator creates fake data, the discriminator tries to detect it. Through this adversarial process, the generator learns to produce realistic outputs.
GBDT: Gradient Boosted Decision Trees — an ensemble method that builds trees sequentially, each correcting the errors of previous trees. XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost are popular implementations — often state-of-the-art for tabular data.
GDPR: General Data Protection Regulation — EU regulation on data protection and privacy. Grants individuals rights over their personal data, including the 'right to explanation' for automated decisions.
GGUF: GPT-Generated Unified Format — a binary file format for storing quantized LLMs for efficient inference on consumer hardware. Used by llama.cpp, Ollama, and LM Studio.
GPT: Generative Pre-trained Transformer — OpenAI's decoder-only LLM series (GPT-3, GPT-3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4o). Pre-trained on next-token prediction, then fine-tuned with instruction data and RLHF.
GPU: Graphics Processing Unit — a processor optimized for parallel matrix operations. Essential for deep learning training and inference. NVIDIA CUDA GPUs are the industry standard.
GRU: Gated Recurrent Unit — a simplified LSTM variant with fewer parameters (combines input and forget gates). Often performs comparably to LSTM with faster training.
Generative AI: AI systems that create new content (text, images, code, music) rather than just classifying or analyzing. LLMs, diffusion models, and GANs are all generative AI.
Git: Distributed version control system tracking changes in source code. Key concepts: commits (snapshots), branches (parallel development), merge/rebase (combining changes), remote (GitHub/GitLab). Created by Linus Torvalds for Linux kernel development.
Git Branching Strategy: A convention for organizing Git branches. GitFlow: main, develop, feature/*, release/*, hotfix/* branches (complex, for scheduled releases). GitHub Flow: main + feature branches (simple, for continuous delivery). Trunk-Based Development: short-lived branches, frequent merges to main.
GitHub: Web-based platform for Git repository hosting, code review, and collaboration. Features: Pull Requests, Issues, Actions (CI/CD), Codespaces (cloud dev environments), Copilot (AI coding assistant). Owned by Microsoft.
GitHub Copilot: An AI-powered code completion tool by GitHub/OpenAI that suggests code in real-time within the IDE. Uses a GPT-based model fine-tuned on public code repositories.
GitLab: Complete DevOps platform providing Git repository management, CI/CD pipelines, container registry, and project management. Available as SaaS (gitlab.com) or self-hosted. Competitor to GitHub with stronger built-in CI/CD capabilities.
Go (Language): A statically typed, compiled language designed at Google. Known for simplicity, fast compilation, built-in concurrency (goroutines + channels), and efficient garbage collection. Used for: cloud infrastructure (Docker, Kubernetes), CLI tools, backend services. Strong standard library.
Google Colab: A free Jupyter notebook environment hosted by Google with optional GPU/TPU access. Popular for prototyping, tutorials, and small-scale fine-tuning. Limited session duration on free tier.
Goroutines: Lightweight threads managed by the Go runtime. Started with `go funcName()`. Much cheaper than OS threads (2KB stack vs 1MB). Multiplexed onto OS threads. Channels provide safe communication between goroutines. Enables the 'don't communicate by sharing memory; share memory by communicating' pattern.
Gradient Accumulation: A technique to simulate larger batch sizes by accumulating gradients over multiple mini-batches before updating weights. Enables training with effective batch sizes that exceed GPU memory limits.
Gradient Descent: The optimization algorithm that iteratively adjusts model parameters in the direction that minimizes the loss function. Variants: SGD, Adam, AdamW. The backbone of neural network training.
GraphQL: A query language for APIs developed by Facebook. Unlike REST (multiple endpoints), GraphQL uses a single endpoint where clients specify exactly what data they need — no over-fetching or under-fetching.
Grounding: The practice of anchoring LLM outputs to verified, real-world data sources rather than relying entirely on model parameters. RAG is a primary grounding technique; citations and attribution are others.
Guardrails: Protective layers around LLM applications that validate inputs and outputs against predefined rules. Detect prompt injection, filter toxic content, enforce formatting, and prevent off-topic responses.
HTML:
HyperText Markup Language — the standard markup language for web pages. Defines structure with elements like
,

, . Current version: HTML5. Works alongside CSS (styling) and JavaScript (behavior).
HTTP: HyperText Transfer Protocol — the foundation of data communication on the web. Clients (browsers) send requests to servers, which respond with status codes (200 OK, 404 Not Found, 500 Internal Server Error).
HTTPS: HTTP Secure — HTTP encrypted with TLS/SSL. Protects data in transit from eavesdropping and tampering. Modern browsers mark non-HTTPS sites as 'Not Secure'. Required for any application handling user data.
Hallucination: When an LLM generates plausible-sounding but factually incorrect information. Caused by the model's probabilistic nature — it predicts likely tokens, not truthful ones. RAG and grounding techniques help mitigate this.
Hashing: Converting input data of any size to a fixed-size string. Properties: deterministic (same input = same output), one-way (can't reverse), avalanche effect (small input change → completely different hash). Algorithms: SHA-256, bcrypt (passwords), MD5 (broken, don't use). Used for: password storage, data integrity, deduplication.
Hoisting: JavaScript's default behavior of moving declarations (not initializations) to the top of their scope during compilation. var is hoisted with undefined; let/const are hoisted but not initialized (Temporal Dead Zone). Function declarations are fully hoisted.
Hugging Face: The central hub for open-source ML — hosts 500K+ pre-trained models, datasets, and demos. The `transformers` library provides a unified API for using and fine-tuning models from any provider.
Hybrid Search: Combining semantic (vector) search with traditional keyword search (BM25) to leverage both conceptual understanding and exact term matching. Often uses reciprocal rank fusion or weighted scoring.
Hyperparameter: A configuration setting external to the model that controls the training process. Examples: learning rate, batch size, number of layers, dropout rate. Set before training and tuned on validation set.
IAM: Identity and Access Management — a framework for managing digital identities and their access to resources. AWS IAM controls who can access AWS services and resources. Involves users, groups, roles, and policies (JSON documents defining permissions).
IAM Policy: A JSON document defining permissions for AWS resources. Structure: Effect (Allow/Deny), Action (API calls), Resource (ARN). Attached to users, groups, or roles. Follows least privilege principle. Example: Allow s3:GetObject on specific bucket.
IAM Role: An AWS identity with permissions policies that determine what it can do. Unlike IAM users (permanent credentials), roles provide temporary credentials via STS. EC2 instances, Lambda functions, and cross-account access assume roles. Best practice: use roles, not access keys.
IDE: Integrated Development Environment — a software application providing comprehensive coding facilities. VS Code (most popular, extensible), IntelliJ/WebStorm (powerful, language-aware), Cursor (AI-native). Features: code completion, debugging, refactoring, terminal integration, version control.
INT4/NF4: 4-bit integer or NormalFloat4 quantization. Reduces model size 8×. NF4 is a data-type optimized for normally distributed weights (used in QLoRA). Enables running large models on consumer hardware.
INT8: 8-bit integer quantization. Reduces model size 4× compared to FP32. Widely supported for inference acceleration on CPUs and GPUs.
IP: Internet Protocol — handles addressing and routing packets across networks. IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses (192.168.1.1), IPv6 uses 128-bit (offering 340 undecillion addresses). The fundamental protocol of the internet.
Idempotency: Property where making the same request multiple times produces the same result as making it once. GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent. POST is NOT idempotent (creates new resources each time). Critical concept for safe API design and retry logic.
Image Segmentation: A computer vision task that classifies each pixel in an image. Semantic segmentation (all cars = one class) vs. instance segmentation (distinguishes individual cars). Models: U-Net, Mask R-CNN, SAM.
Index (Database): A data structure (typically B-tree) that improves the speed of data retrieval operations. Like a book's index, it allows finding rows without scanning the entire table. Tradeoff: faster reads, slower writes (index must be updated). Common on primary keys, foreign keys, and frequently queried columns.
Inference: The process of using a trained model to make predictions on new data. Contrasts with training. Inference servers (TGI, vLLM, Triton) optimize for throughput, latency, and resource efficiency.
Infrastructure as Code: Managing infrastructure through machine-readable definition files rather than manual configuration. Tools: Terraform (multi-cloud, HCL), CloudFormation (AWS-native, JSON/YAML), CDK (programming languages → CloudFormation), Pulumi (programming languages, multi-cloud).
Instruction Tuning: Fine-tuning a base model on (instruction, response) pairs to teach it to follow instructions. Transforms a raw next-token predictor into a helpful assistant. Key step in creating chat models.
JSON: JavaScript Object Notation — a lightweight, text-based data interchange format. Human-readable, language-independent. The standard format for REST API request/response bodies. Example: {"name": "John", "age": 30}.
JSX:
JavaScript XML — a syntax extension for JavaScript that looks like HTML. Used with React to describe UI components. Compiled to React.createElement() calls by Babel or TypeScript. Example:
{name}
.
JWT: JSON Web Token — a compact, URL-safe token format for securely transmitting claims between parties. Three parts: header (algorithm), payload (claims), signature (HMAC/RSA). Used for stateless authentication. Critically: the payload is base64-encoded, NOT encrypted — never store secrets in it.
JavaScript: High-level, interpreted programming language conforming to the ECMAScript specification. The language of the web — runs in every browser. Event-driven, prototype-based, with first-class functions. Runtime environments: browsers, Node.js, Deno, Bun.
Jupyter Notebook: An interactive web-based computational environment combining code, text, and visualizations. The de facto standard for data exploration, ML experimentation, and tutorials in Python.
K-Means: A popular clustering algorithm that partitions data into K clusters by iteratively assigning points to nearest centroid and updating centroids. Fast but assumes spherical clusters of similar size.
KV Cache: Key-Value Cache — the stored attention key and value tensors from previously processed tokens, used to avoid recomputation during autoregressive generation. Memory bottleneck for long sequences.
Kanban: Agile methodology focused on visualizing workflow and limiting work in progress. Board columns represent stages. Cards represent work items. WIP limits prevent bottlenecks. No prescribed timeboxes — continuous delivery. Complementary to Scrum (Scrumban).
Kubernetes: K8s — an open-source container orchestration platform. Automates deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. Key concepts: Pods (smallest deployable unit), Services (networking), Deployments (desired state), Namespaces (isolation).
LCEL: LangChain Expression Language — a declarative syntax using the pipe (|) operator for composing LangChain components into chains. Enables streaming, async, and parallel execution.
LIME: Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations — an explainability technique that approximates a complex model locally with a simpler, interpretable model (e.g., linear model) around a specific prediction.
LLM: Large Language Model — a deep neural network (typically a Transformer) trained on massive text corpora to understand and generate human-like text. Examples: GPT-4, Claude, Llama, Gemini.
LSTM: Long Short-Term Memory — an RNN variant with gating mechanisms (input, forget, output gates) that better capture long-range dependencies. Addresses vanishing gradient problem of vanilla RNNs.
Lambda: AWS Lambda — serverless compute service. Upload code, it runs in response to events (API Gateway requests, S3 uploads, DynamoDB changes) and auto-scales. Pay only for compute time used. Supports many runtimes including Node.js, Python, and custom runtimes via containers.
LangChain: An open-source framework for building LLM-powered applications. Provides abstractions for chains, agents, tools, memory, and retrieval. Supports Python and JavaScript/TypeScript.
LangGraph: A library built on LangChain for creating stateful, multi-actor agentic applications as graphs. Supports cycles, branching, and human-in-the-loop workflows.
Learning Rate: A hyperparameter controlling how much model weights are updated in each training step. Too high: unstable training, may diverge. Too low: slow convergence. Critical for fine-tuning success.
Linter: A static code analysis tool that flags programming errors, bugs, stylistic issues, and suspicious constructs. ESLint (JavaScript/TypeScript), Pylint/Ruff (Python), Clippy (Rust). Can auto-fix many issues. Enforces coding standards across teams. Configured via config files (e.g., .eslintrc.js).
LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation — a parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) method that inserts small trainable adapter matrices into frozen model layers, enabling fine-tuning of large models with dramatically less GPU memory.
Load Balancer (AWS): AWS offers ALB (Application, HTTP/HTTPS, path/host routing, WebSocket), NLB (Network, TCP/UDP/TLS, ultra-low latency, static IP), and GWLB (Gateway, for third-party virtual appliances). ALB is the most common — integrates with ECS, Lambda, and supports redirects and fixed responses.
Logistic Regression: A linear classification model that predicts probabilities using the sigmoid function. Despite its name, it's for classification (not regression). Baseline model for binary classification tasks.
Loss Function: A mathematical function measuring the difference between model predictions and ground truth labels. The model minimizes this during training. Cross-entropy loss is standard for classification and language modeling.
MAE: Mean Absolute Error — a regression metric measuring average absolute prediction error. Less sensitive to outliers than RMSE. Simple to interpret: average error magnitude.
MCP: Model Context Protocol — an open protocol introduced by Anthropic for standardizing how AI agents connect to external tools and data sources. Enables plug-and-play tool integration across different agent frameworks.
MFA: Multi-Factor Authentication — requiring two or more verification factors: something you know (password), something you have (phone/security key), something you are (fingerprint/face). Dramatically reduces account takeover risk. TOTP is the most common implementation.
MIME Type: Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions type — a label identifying the format of a file sent over the internet. text/html, application/json, image/png. Web servers and browsers use MIME types to determine how to process or display content.
ML: Machine Learning — a subset of AI where systems learn patterns from data without being explicitly programmed. Encompasses supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning.
MLOps: Machine Learning Operations — the practice of applying DevOps principles to ML systems. Encompasses CI/CD for models, monitoring, versioning (data + models), feature stores, and automated retraining pipelines.
MRR: Mean Reciprocal Rank — a retrieval metric: 1/rank of the first relevant document, averaged over queries. Higher is better. MRR=1 means the first result was always relevant.
MTEB: Massive Text Embedding Benchmark — the standard leaderboard for comparing embedding model quality across diverse tasks (classification, clustering, semantic search, summarization).
MVC: Model-View-Controller — an architectural pattern separating an application into three components: Model (data/business logic), View (UI presentation), Controller (handles input, updates model and view). Foundation of frameworks like Rails, Django, and Express.
Message Queue: A buffer that stores messages until they can be processed. Senders put messages on the queue; receivers pull and process them asynchronously. AWS SQS, RabbitMQ, Apache Kafka. Decouples services and handles back-pressure. Point-to-point (queue) vs publish-subscribe (topic).
Metadata Filtering: Pre-filtering or post-filtering vector search results based on metadata fields (date, author, document type). Ensures retrieved chunks match not just semantically but also on structured criteria.
Microservices: Architectural style where an application is composed of small, independent services communicating via APIs. Each service owns its data and can be deployed independently. Benefits: scalability, fault isolation, technology diversity. Challenges: distributed system complexity, eventual consistency.
Middleware: Functions that have access to the request and response objects in an application's request-response cycle. In Express: app.use(middleware). Can modify req/res, end the cycle, or call next(). Used for logging, authentication, CORS, body parsing, error handling.
Migrations: A way to version-control database schema changes. Each migration is a script that modifies the database structure (add table, add column, add index). Can be rolled forward (apply) and backward (revert). Tools: Prisma Migrate, Alembic (Python), Flyway (Java).
Model Drift: Degradation of model performance over time because the statistical properties of the input data change relative to training data. Types: data drift (input distribution changes), concept drift (relationship between inputs and target changes).
Model Interpretability: The degree to which a human can understand why a model made a specific prediction. Critical for high-stakes domains (healthcare, finance, criminal justice).
Model Merging: Combining the weights of multiple fine-tuned models into a single model. Techniques: SLERP (spherical linear interpolation), TIES, DARE. Can combine different skills without additional training.
Model Registry: A central repository for tracking, versioning, and managing trained ML models. Stores model artifacts, metadata, and deployment status. Enables reproducible model promotion (staging → production).
MongoDB: A popular document-oriented NoSQL database. Stores data as flexible BSON (Binary JSON) documents in collections. Schema-less design. Features: aggregation pipeline, indexing, sharding (horizontal scaling), and replication. Default choice in the MEAN/MERN stack.
Monorepo: A single repository containing multiple projects/packages. Tools: Nx, Turborepo, Lerna. Benefits: shared code, unified CI/CD, atomic cross-project changes. Challenges: larger clone size, complex tooling. Used by Google, Meta, and many large orgs.
Multi-Query Retrieval: Generating multiple diverse versions of a user query using an LLM, retrieving documents for each variant, and combining deduplicated results. Improves recall for ambiguous or complex queries.
NACL: Network Access Control List — a stateless firewall at the subnet level in AWS VPC. Rules are evaluated in order (lowest rule number first). Unlike Security Groups, NACLs control traffic entering and leaving subnets. Default NACL allows all traffic; custom NACLs deny all by default.
NDCG: Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain — a ranking metric that accounts for position (higher-ranked items weighted more) and graded relevance (not just relevant/irrelevant). Standard for search quality evaluation.
NER: Named Entity Recognition — an NLP task that identifies and classifies named entities (people, organizations, locations, dates) in text. A core information extraction building block.
NLP: Natural Language Processing — the AI subfield focused on enabling computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language. Includes tasks like translation, summarization, and sentiment analysis.
NLU: Natural Language Understanding — the NLP subfield focused on machine reading comprehension: extracting meaning, intent, entities, and relationships from text.
Neural Network: A computational model inspired by biological neurons, consisting of layers of interconnected nodes. Each connection has a weight; forward propagation computes predictions; backpropagation updates weights.
Next.js: React framework providing SSR, SSG, file-based routing, API routes, and image optimization. Created by Vercel. Supports both Pages Router and newer App Router (React Server Components). The most popular React meta-framework.
NoSQL: Non-relational databases optimized for specific data models. Types: document (MongoDB), key-value (Redis), column-family (Cassandra), graph (Neo4j). Trade off ACID guarantees for scalability and flexibility. Often used with unstructured or rapidly changing data.
Node.js: JavaScript runtime built on Chrome's V8 JavaScript engine. Enables server-side JavaScript with an event-driven, non-blocking I/O model. Package ecosystem: npm (largest package registry in the world). Used for APIs, real-time apps, and tooling.
Normalization: Scaling input features to a common range (e.g., 0-1 or mean=0, std=1) to help models converge faster and avoid features with larger scales dominating the learning process.
OAuth: Open Authorization — a standard for access delegation. Allows users to grant third-party apps limited access to their resources without sharing passwords. Flows: Authorization Code (most secure, for server-side apps), Implicit (deprecated), PKCE (for mobile/SPA).
OAuth 2.0 Flow: Authentication flows: Authorization Code (server-side apps, most secure), PKCE (mobile/SPA, prevents interception), Client Credentials (machine-to-machine), Device Code (input-constrained devices like TVs). Implicit flow is deprecated. The Authorization Code + PKCE is the recommended flow for most use cases.
OIDC: OpenID Connect — an identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0. Adds authentication (who you are) to OAuth's authorization (what you can access). Returns an ID Token (JWT) with user claims. Used by 'Sign in with Google/Facebook/GitHub' flows.
ONNX: Open Neural Network Exchange — an open format for representing ML models, enabling interoperability between frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow, scikit-learn) and deployment runtimes.
ONNX Runtime: A cross-platform inference engine for ONNX models. Optimizes model execution across different hardware (CPU, GPU, edge devices) with minimal configuration.
OOP: Object-Oriented Programming — a paradigm based on objects containing data (properties) and behavior (methods). Four pillars: Encapsulation (hiding state), Abstraction (simplifying complexity), Inheritance (is-a relationships), Polymorphism (same interface, different implementations).
ORM: Object-Relational Mapping — a technique for converting data between relational databases and object-oriented programming languages. Tools: Prisma, TypeORM, Sequelize (JS/TS), Hibernate (Java), SQLAlchemy (Python). Eliminates manual SQL for common operations.
ORM vs Raw SQL: ORM abstracts database operations into object method calls — easier, safer (prevents SQL injection), database-agnostic. Raw SQL gives full control, better performance for complex queries, and access to database-specific features. Many production systems use both: ORM for CRUD, raw SQL for complex reporting.
Object Detection: A computer vision task combining object localization (bounding box) with classification. Models: YOLO (fast, single-pass), Faster R-CNN (two-stage, more accurate).
Ollama: A tool for running open-source LLMs locally with a simple CLI and REST API. Supports GGUF models, custom Modelfiles, and GPU acceleration. Popular for local development and privacy-sensitive deployments.
One-Hot Encoding: Converting categorical variables into binary vectors where exactly one element is 1 ('hot') and the rest are 0. Necessary for feeding categorical data into many ML algorithms.
Overfitting: When a model memorizes training data instead of learning generalizable patterns. Symptoms: low training loss but poor test/validation performance. Mitigated by regularization, dropout, and early stopping.
PCA: Principal Component Analysis — a dimensionality reduction technique that projects data onto directions of maximum variance. Used for visualization, noise reduction, and feature compression.
PEFT: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning — a family of techniques (LoRA, Adapters, Prefix Tuning) that fine-tune only a small fraction of model parameters, reducing compute and storage costs.
PII: Personally Identifiable Information — any data that can identify an individual (name, email, SSN, IP address). Must be protected, anonymized, or excluded from training data for compliance.
Parent Document Retriever: A retrieval pattern where small chunks are used for embedding/search, but the full parent document (larger surrounding text) is returned for the LLM. Balances retrieval precision with response context.
Perplexity: A metric evaluating language model quality — measures how 'surprised' the model is by a text sequence. Lower perplexity = better prediction. e^loss for cross-entropy loss.
Perplexity (Metric): Measures how well a language model predicts a text sample. Mathematically: exp(cross-entropy loss). Lower is better. A perplexity of 10 means the model is as confused as if choosing uniformly from 10 options.
Pipeline Parallelism: A model parallelism technique where different layers of a model are placed on different GPUs, with activations flowing between them. Enables training models too large for a single GPU.
PostgreSQL: Advanced open-source relational database. Features: ACID compliance, complex queries (CTEs, window functions), JSON support, full-text search, extensions (PostGIS for geospatial). Known for reliability, standards compliance, and extensibility.
Precision: Of all items the model predicted as positive, what fraction were actually positive? TP / (TP + FP). High precision means few false positives.
Promises: A JavaScript object representing the eventual completion (or failure) of an async operation. States: pending, fulfilled, rejected. then() and catch() handle results. async/await is syntactic sugar on top of Promises. Introduced in ES6 (2015).
Prompt Caching: Storing and reusing the KV-cache computation for frequently used prompt prefixes. Reduces latency and cost when system prompts or few-shot examples repeat across requests.
Prompt Engineering: The practice of designing and optimizing input prompts to elicit desired behaviors from LLMs. Techniques include few-shot examples, chain-of-thought, role prompting, and structured output formatting.
Prompt Injection: A security vulnerability where malicious user input attempts to override or manipulate the system prompt or model behavior. Can be direct ('ignore instructions') or indirect (embedded in retrieved documents).
Props: Short for 'properties'. Read-only data passed from a parent component to a child component in React. Flow is one-way (parent → child). Children cannot modify props. Functions can be passed as props for child-to-parent communication (callback pattern).
Pub/Sub: Publish/Subscribe — a messaging pattern where senders (publishers) send messages to a topic, and multiple receivers (subscribers) receive them. Decouples producers and consumers. AWS SNS, Google Pub/Sub, Redis pub/sub. Ideal for fan-out scenarios.
Pull Request: A request to merge code changes from one branch to another (typically feature → main). Enables code review, discussion, and CI checks before merging. GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket all support PRs (called Merge Requests in GitLab). Best practice: small, focused PRs with clear descriptions.
QLoRA: Quantized LoRA — combines 4-bit model quantization with LoRA adapters, allowing fine-tuning of very large models (e.g., 65B parameters) on a single consumer GPU.
Quantization: The process of reducing the numerical precision of model weights (e.g., from FP32 to INT8 or INT4) to decrease model size and increase inference speed, with minimal accuracy loss.
RAG: Retrieval-Augmented Generation — an architectural pattern where an LLM retrieves relevant documents from a knowledge base before generating a response, grounding answers in real data rather than relying solely on model weights.
RBAC: Role-Based Access Control — assigning permissions to roles rather than individual users. Users are assigned roles (admin, editor, viewer), each role has specific permissions. Simplifies access management at scale. Standard in enterprise applications.
RDS: Amazon Relational Database Service — managed relational database service supporting MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, and SQL Server. Handles backups, patching, and replication. Multi-AZ for high availability. Read replicas for read scaling.
REST: REpresentational State Transfer — an architectural style for designing networked applications. Uses stateless HTTP requests, standard methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE), and typically returns JSON responses.
REST API: An API that follows REST principles: stateless, client-server, cacheable, layered, with uniform interface. The most common way web and mobile apps communicate with backend services.
RLHF: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback — a technique for aligning LLM behavior with human preferences. Process: 1) Collect human preference comparisons, 2) Train a reward model, 3) Fine-tune the LLM with PPO to maximize reward.
RMSE: Root Mean Square Error — a regression metric measuring the standard deviation of prediction errors. In same units as the target variable. Penalizes large errors more than MAE.
RNN: Recurrent Neural Network — a neural network architecture designed for sequential data, maintaining a hidden state that captures temporal dependencies. Largely replaced by Transformers for most NLP tasks.
ROC AUC: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve — measures a binary classifier's ability to discriminate between classes across all thresholds. 1.0 = perfect, 0.5 = random.
Random Forest: An ensemble method combining multiple decision trees trained on random subsets of data and features. Reduces overfitting compared to single trees. Robust, interpretable baseline for tabular data.
Rate Limiting: Restricting the number of requests a client can make in a time window. Prevents abuse, manages costs, ensures fair usage. Implementations: token bucket, sliding window, fixed window. Headers: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset. 429 Too Many Requests response.
Re-ranker: A second-stage model that re-scores retrieved documents based on fine-grained relevance to the query. Typically a cross-encoder that processes query-document pairs together for more accurate ranking.
ReAct: Reasoning + Acting — a prompt pattern where the model alternates between reasoning (thought), taking an action (tool call), and observing the result. Foundation of AI agent architectures.
ReLU: Rectified Linear Unit — activation function f(x)=max(0,x). Computationally simple, avoids vanishing gradient for positive inputs. Standard in CNNs. Variant: GELU (used in Transformers).
React: JavaScript library for building user interfaces, developed by Facebook. Component-based architecture with a virtual DOM for efficient rendering. Uses JSX syntax and one-way data flow. The most popular frontend framework as of 2024.
React Context: A way to pass data through the component tree without manually passing props at every level. Create: React.createContext(). Provide: <Context.Provider value={...}>. Consume: useContext(Context) or <Context.Consumer>. Good for: theme, auth, locale. Not a replacement for state management at scale.
React Hooks: Functions that let you use state and other React features without writing a class. useState (local state), useEffect (side effects), useContext (context API), useRef (mutable ref), useMemo/useCallback (performance optimization). Introduced in React 16.8 (2019). Rules: only call at top level, only from React functions.
Recall: Of all actual positive items, what fraction did the model correctly identify? TP / (TP + FN). High recall means few false negatives. Also called sensitivity.
Redis: In-memory data structure store used as database, cache, and message broker. Supports strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams. Sub-millisecond latency. Use cases: session store, rate limiting, leaderboards, pub/sub messaging, job queues.
Redux: A predictable state container for JavaScript apps. Single store holds entire state. Actions describe what happened. Reducers specify how state changes. Uses immutability. Redux Toolkit (RTK) simplifies setup. Alternatives: Zustand (lighter), Jotai (atomic), MobX (reactive). Often overkill for small-mid apps.
Regression: A supervised learning task of predicting continuous numerical values (e.g., house prices, temperature). Common metrics: MSE, RMSE, MAE, R-squared.
Regularization: Techniques to prevent overfitting by adding constraints to model complexity. Includes L1/L2 weight penalties, dropout (randomly disabling neurons during training), and early stopping.
Reinforcement Learning: ML paradigm where an agent learns by interacting with an environment, receiving rewards or penalties for actions. Used in game playing (AlphaGo), robotics, and LLM alignment (RLHF).
Replication: Copying data from one database server (primary) to others (replicas). Read replicas handle read queries, primary handles writes. Improves read scalability and availability. Synchronous: primary waits for replica confirmation. Asynchronous: faster but potential data loss on failover.
Responsible AI: A framework for developing AI systems that are fair, transparent, accountable, privacy-preserving, and aligned with human values. Encompasses ethics, safety, and governance.
Retry Pattern: Automatically retrying failed operations with delay between attempts. Exponential backoff: wait time doubles after each failure (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s...). Jitter: random variation to prevent thundering herd. Max retries prevents infinite loops. AWS SDK implements this by default.
Route 53: AWS's DNS service. Features: domain registration, DNS routing (Latency-based, Geolocation, Geoproximity, Weighted, Failover), health checks, and private hosted zones for internal VPC DNS. Integrates with CloudFront, ELB, and S3 for seamless routing.
S3: Amazon Simple Storage Service — object storage with 99.999999999% (11 9's) durability. Stores any amount of data as objects in buckets. Features: versioning, lifecycle policies, static website hosting, event notifications. Storage classes: Standard, Intelligent-Tiering, Glacier (archive).
S3 Presigned URL: A time-limited URL granting temporary access to a private S3 object. Generated using the bucket owner's credentials. Anyone with the URL can access the object during the validity period. Common for secure file downloads and direct browser uploads to S3.
SDK: Software Development Kit — a collection of tools, libraries, and documentation for building applications on a platform. AWS SDK enables programmatic access to AWS services from your language of choice. Stripe SDK simplifies payment integration. SDK wraps raw API calls with idiomatic code.
SFT: Supervised Fine-Tuning — the initial fine-tuning step where a pre-trained LLM is trained on instruction-response pairs to learn the chat/assistant format before preference alignment.
SHAP: SHapley Additive exPlanations — a game-theoretic approach to explaining model predictions by computing each feature's contribution. Widely used for model interpretability.
SLERP: Spherical Linear Interpolation — a technique for smoothly blending between two sets of model weights on the unit hypersphere. Used in model merging to combine capabilities of different fine-tuned models.
SOLID: Five design principles for maintainable OOP code: Single Responsibility (one reason to change), Open/Closed (open for extension, closed for modification), Liskov Substitution (subtypes must be substitutable), Interface Segregation (small focused interfaces), Dependency Inversion (depend on abstractions).
SPA: Single Page Application — a web app that loads a single HTML page and dynamically updates content via JavaScript. No full page reloads. Frameworks: React (with React Router), Angular, Vue. Tradeoff: slower initial load, better UX after.
SQL: Structured Query Language — the standard language for managing relational databases. Operations: SELECT (query), INSERT (create), UPDATE (modify), DELETE (remove). Used by PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, SQL Server, and many others.
SQLi: SQL Injection — an attack where malicious SQL statements are inserted into application queries via user input. Example: ' OR '1'='1. Prevention: parameterized queries/prepared statements, ORM usage, input validation, least privilege database accounts.
SSG: Static Site Generation — pre-rendering all HTML pages at build time. Extremely fast, great for SEO, can be hosted on CDN. Next.js supports SSG with getStaticProps. Ideal for blogs, documentation, and marketing sites.
SSH: Secure Shell — a cryptographic network protocol for secure remote login and command execution. Uses public-key cryptography for authentication. Port 22. Used daily by developers to access remote servers. SSH keys (~/.ssh/id_rsa) are more secure than passwords.
SSL: Secure Sockets Layer — the predecessor to TLS. Now deprecated due to security vulnerabilities. The term 'SSL' is still colloquially used to refer to TLS certificates.
SSO: Single Sign-On — allows users to authenticate once and access multiple applications. Uses a central identity provider (IdP). Protocols: SAML (enterprise), OIDC (modern web), Kerberos (corporate networks). Examples: Okta, Azure AD, Auth0.
SSR: Server-Side Rendering — generating HTML on the server for each request rather than in the browser. Improves SEO and initial page load speed. Next.js (React) and Nuxt (Vue) provide SSR. Can be combined with client-side hydration.
SVM: Support Vector Machine — a classification algorithm that finds the optimal hyperplane separating classes with maximum margin. Effective in high-dimensional spaces; uses kernel trick for non-linear boundaries.
Scrum: Agile framework with fixed-length sprints (usually 2 weeks). Roles: Product Owner (priorities), Scrum Master (facilitator), Development Team. Ceremonies: Sprint Planning, Daily Standup, Sprint Review, Retrospective. Artifacts: Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, Increment.
Security Group: A virtual firewall controlling inbound and outbound traffic to AWS resources (EC2, RDS, Lambda in VPC). Stateful: if inbound is allowed, return traffic is automatically allowed. Rules specify protocol, port range, and source/destination (CIDR or security group ID).
Self-Attention: A type of attention where every token in a sequence attends to every other token in the same sequence, capturing intra-sequence relationships. The core operation in Transformer models.
Self-Query: An LLM-powered retrieval technique where the model converts a natural language query into a structured query (semantic + metadata filters). Enables 'find papers about RAG from 2024' type queries.
Semantic Chunking: A chunking strategy that splits documents at natural semantic boundaries (paragraphs, sections) rather than fixed token counts. Often produces better retrieval quality than naive character splitting.
Semantic Search: Search that understands meaning and intent, not just keyword matching. Uses embeddings to find content that is conceptually similar to the query, even when exact words don't match.
Semantic Versioning: Versioning scheme: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH (e.g., 2.1.3). MAJOR: breaking changes. MINOR: new features (backward-compatible). PATCH: bug fixes. ^1.2.3 means >=1.2.3 and <2.0.0. ~1.2.3 means >=1.2.3 and <1.3.0. Pre-release suffixes: alpha, beta, rc.
Serialization: Converting an object or data structure into a format that can be stored (file) or transmitted (network) and later reconstructed. In JS: JSON.stringify() serializes, JSON.parse() deserializes. In Python: pickle, json modules.
Serverless: Cloud computing model where the cloud provider dynamically manages server allocation. Developers only write and deploy code. AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, GCP Cloud Functions. Pay-per-invocation pricing. Auto-scaling built in. Cold starts are the main challenge.
Shadow Deployment: Running a new model version in parallel with the production model, logging its predictions without affecting users. Allows safe evaluation on live traffic before promotion.
Sharding: Horizontal partitioning — splitting a database across multiple servers (shards), each holding a subset of data. Improves scalability and performance. Sharding key determines which shard holds which data. Challenges: cross-shard queries, rebalancing, joins. Used by MongoDB, Vitess (MySQL), and Citus (PostgreSQL).
State: Mutable data managed within a component that determines its behavior and rendering. Unlike props, state is internal and can change. State changes trigger re-renders. useState for local state. Lift state up when multiple components need it. State management libraries for global state.
Sticky Session: A load balancer technique that routes a user's requests to the same backend server. Required when session data is stored in server memory (not shared). Application Load Balancers support cookie-based stickiness. Better approach: store sessions in Redis/DynamoDB (stateless servers).
Streaming: Returning LLM output token-by-token as it's generated, rather than waiting for the full response. Critical for UX — reduces perceived latency from seconds to milliseconds (TTFT).
Strong Consistency: A consistency model where after a write completes, all subsequent reads will see that write. Most RDBMS provide strong consistency. In distributed systems: requires coordination (Paxos/Raft), increases latency. DynamoDB offers strongly consistent reads as an option.
Structured Output: Constraining LLM output to conform to a specified schema (JSON, Pydantic model, regex). Prevents parsing errors and enables reliable integration with downstream systems.
Supervised Learning: ML paradigm where models learn from labeled data — input-output pairs. Includes classification (predicting categories) and regression (predicting continuous values).
System Prompt: A special message (typically the first in a conversation) that sets the LLM's behavior, role, constraints, and tone. Treated with higher priority than user messages in most model APIs.
T4 GPU: NVIDIA T4 — a data center GPU with 16GB VRAM, optimized for inference. Free tier GPU on Google Colab and common in cloud instances. Suitable for running quantized 7B-13B parameter models.
TCP: Transmission Control Protocol — connection-oriented, reliable transport protocol. Guarantees ordered delivery of data with error checking via acknowledgments, retransmission, and flow control. Most web traffic (HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2) runs over TCP.
TDD: Test-Driven Development — write failing tests first, then write code to pass them, then refactor. Red-Green-Refactor cycle. Ensures code is testable and tests actually validate behavior. Challenges: requires discipline, can slow initial development.
TGI: Text Generation Inference — Hugging Face's production-grade inference server. Features continuous batching, tensor parallelism, watermarked outputs, and optimized transformer kernels.
TIES Merging: Trim, Elect Sign, and Merge — a model merging technique that resolves interference between models: trims low-magnitude changes, resolves sign conflicts, and merges only aligned parameters.
TLS: Transport Layer Security — the cryptographic protocol that secures HTTPS connections. Handles encryption, authentication (via certificates), and data integrity. TLS 1.3 is the current standard (faster handshake, forward secrecy).
TTFT: Time To First Token — the latency from when a request is sent to when the first response token appears. Key UX metric for chat applications; streaming keeps this under ~200ms.
Temperature: A parameter controlling the randomness of LLM output. Low temperature (~0.1) produces deterministic, focused responses. High temperature (~0.8-1.0) increases creativity and diversity by flattening the token probability distribution.
Tensor Core: Specialized GPU hardware units (NVIDIA) that accelerate mixed-precision matrix multiplication. Dramatically speed up training and inference on modern NVIDIA GPUs.
Tensor Parallelism: A model parallelism technique that splits individual layer weights across multiple GPUs. Each GPU computes a portion of the layer, then communicates results. Used with pipeline parallelism for very large models.
Terraform: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tool by HashiCorp. Uses declarative HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language) to define and provision infrastructure across multiple cloud providers. Maintains state files to track managed resources.
Test Set: A final held-out portion of data used only once to estimate real-world model performance. Must never be used during training or hyperparameter tuning.
Token: The basic unit of text processed by an LLM — can be a word, subword, or character. Tokenization splits input text into tokens; models have a maximum token limit (context window).
Token Counting: Calculating the number of tokens in a text to manage context window usage and estimate API costs. Different models use different tokenizers; tools like tiktoken provide accurate counts.
Tokenization: The process of converting raw text into tokens (numerical IDs) that the model can process. Common algorithms: BPE (Byte-Pair Encoding), WordPiece, SentencePiece.
Tool Use: The capability of an AI agent to call external functions, APIs, or services. Tools extend the agent beyond text generation — enabling calculations, web searches, database queries, and code execution.
TorchScript: A way to serialize PyTorch models for production deployment. Converts eager-mode Python models to an optimized, Python-independent intermediate representation.
Training: The process of learning model parameters from data by minimizing a loss function using optimization algorithms like gradient descent. Typically requires significant GPU compute.
Training Set: The portion of data used to fit the model parameters. The model directly learns from this data.
Transfer Learning: Leveraging knowledge from a pre-trained model (trained on a large general dataset) and adapting it to a specific downstream task with less data and compute. Foundation of modern NLP and CV.
Transformer: The neural network architecture introduced in 'Attention Is All You Need' (2017) that replaced RNNs. Uses self-attention mechanisms to process sequences in parallel. Foundation of GPT, BERT, and all modern LLMs.
Transpiler: A tool that converts source code from one language or version to another at the same abstraction level. Examples: TypeScript → JavaScript, ES6+ → ES5. Differs from a compiler (source → machine code) by keeping the source at the same level of abstraction.
TypeScript: A strongly-typed superset of JavaScript developed by Microsoft. Adds static type checking, interfaces, generics, and enums. Compiled to plain JavaScript. Dramatically improves code quality and developer experience in large codebases.
UDP: User Datagram Protocol — connectionless, lightweight transport protocol. No guarantee of delivery or ordering. Used when speed matters more than reliability: DNS lookups, VoIP, gaming, streaming. HTTP/3 uses QUIC over UDP.
Underfitting: When a model is too simple to capture the underlying patterns in the data. Symptoms: high error on both training and test sets. Fixed by increasing model capacity or training longer.
Unsupervised Learning: ML paradigm where models find patterns in unlabeled data. Includes clustering (K-Means, DBSCAN), dimensionality reduction (PCA, t-SNE), and anomaly detection.
VPC: Virtual Private Cloud — an isolated section of the AWS cloud where you launch resources. Configure IP ranges, subnets (public/private), route tables, and network gateways. NAT Gateways allow private subnets to access the internet. Fundamental to AWS networking and security.
VRAM: Video Random Access Memory — the dedicated memory on a GPU. Determines the maximum model size that can be loaded. 24GB (RTX 4090) can run 13B-parameter models with quantization.
Validation Set: A held-out portion of data used to tune hyperparameters and detect overfitting during training. Never used for parameter updates, but guides decisions about when to stop training.
Vector Database: A specialized database optimized for storing and querying embedding vectors. Enables fast similarity search (nearest neighbor) for RAG, semantic search, and recommendation systems. Examples: ChromaDB, Pinecone, Weaviate, Milvus.
Vector Store: Synonym for Vector Database. A storage system that indexes dense vector embeddings and supports approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search for semantic retrieval.
Virtual DOM: An in-memory representation of the real DOM. React maintains two virtual DOM trees (previous and current). Reconciliation (diffing) finds minimal changes. Only the differences are applied to the real DOM (batching). This is why React is fast — real DOM manipulation is expensive.
Vite: A modern build tool created by Evan You (Vue.js creator). Uses native ES modules during development (instant HMR) and Rollup for production builds. Much faster than Webpack for dev. Framework-agnostic but especially popular with Vue and React.
WebSocket: A protocol providing full-duplex (two-way) communication over a single TCP connection. Used for real-time features like chat, live notifications, and collaborative editing. Upgrades from HTTP via the 101 Switching Protocols response.
Webhook: A user-defined HTTP callback — a way for one application to provide real-time data to another when an event occurs. GitHub sends a POST to your URL when code is pushed. Contrasts with polling (repeatedly checking for changes). Requires a publicly accessible endpoint.
Webpack: A static module bundler for JavaScript applications. Transforms and bundles JS, CSS, images, and other assets. Creates a dependency graph and outputs optimized bundles. Configured via webpack.config.js. Competitors: Vite (faster dev server), esbuild, Rollup, Parcel.
X-Ray: AWS distributed tracing service for analyzing and debugging microservice applications. Shows request paths, latency distribution, and error rates. Instruments applications with SDK; visualizes service map and trace details. Works with EC2, ECS, Lambda, and API Gateway.
XGBoost: Extreme Gradient Boosting — a highly optimized GBDT library known for speed and performance. Dominant in Kaggle competitions and production ML for structured/tabular data.
XSS: Cross-Site Scripting — a security vulnerability where attackers inject malicious scripts into web pages viewed by others. Types: Stored (persisted in DB), Reflected (in URL parameters), DOM-based. Prevention: sanitize user input, use Content-Security-Policy headers, escape output.
YAML: YAML Ain't Markup Language — a human-readable data serialization format. Uses indentation (not braces) for structure. Common in configuration files: Docker Compose, Kubernetes manifests, CI/CD configs, Ansible playbooks. Strict about indentation (2 spaces standard). Superset of JSON.
YOLO: You Only Look Once — a real-time object detection algorithm that predicts bounding boxes and class probabilities in a single forward pass through the network. Known for speed.
Zero-Shot Prompting: Asking an LLM to perform a task without providing any examples — relying entirely on the model's pre-trained knowledge and instruction-following ability.
async/await: Syntactic sugar over Promises in JavaScript. async functions always return a Promise. await pauses execution until the Promise resolves. Makes asynchronous code read like synchronous code. Error handling uses try/catch instead of .catch().
deadlock: A situation where two or more processes are waiting for each other to release resources, creating a circular dependency. In databases: transaction A locks row 1 and wants row 2; transaction B locks row 2 and wants row 1. Prevention: acquire locks in consistent order, use timeouts.
gRPC: A high-performance RPC framework by Google using Protocol Buffers and HTTP/2. Supports bidirectional streaming and is often used for microservice-to-microservice communication.
gRPC vs REST: gRPC uses Protocol Buffers (binary, smaller, faster) over HTTP/2 with bi-directional streaming. REST uses JSON (text, human-readable) over HTTP/1.1. gRPC is preferred for internal microservice communication; REST for public APIs and browser accessibility.
load_balancing: Distributing incoming network traffic across multiple servers to ensure high availability and reliability. Types: Application Load Balancer (L7, HTTP-aware), Network Load Balancer (L4, TCP/UDP), Global Server Load Balancing (multi-region). Algorithms: round-robin, least connections.
npm: Node Package Manager — the world's largest software registry. Used to install, share, and manage JavaScript packages. package.json defines dependencies and scripts. npm install downloads dependencies to node_modules/.
race_condition: A bug where the output depends on the unpredictable timing or sequencing of events. Common in: async code (two requests modifying the same record), multi-threaded code (shared state without locks). Solutions: locks, transactions (SELECT FOR UPDATE), atomic operations, queue-based processing.
stateless vs stateful: Stateless: each request is independent, server doesn't remember past requests (REST ideal). Stateful: server maintains session state between requests. Stateless enables horizontal scaling. Stateful requires sticky sessions or shared state storage.
useEffect: React Hook for performing side effects: data fetching, subscriptions, DOM manipulation. Runs after render. Second argument (dependency array) controls when it re-runs: [] = once on mount, [dep] = when dep changes, no array = every render. Cleanup function returned for teardown logic.
useState: React Hook for adding local state to functional components. useState(initialValue) returns [value, setter]. Calling setter triggers re-render. Can hold any type. Uses batching: multiple setState calls in event handlers are batched into a single re-render (React 18).
vLLM: A high-throughput LLM serving system with PagedAttention for efficient KV cache management. Supports continuous batching and tensor parallelism. Popular for self-hosted LLM APIs.
